region_of_origin: Global — widely consumed across Southeast Asia, East Asia, and Europe

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept identified for raw pig heart as a food item; SNOMED CT coverage for porcine organ meats as food is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of raw pig heart is approximately 85g, equivalent to a palm-sized portion before cooking, commonly used in braised dishes, soups, or stir-fries.

nutrient_source.title: Pig heart, raw — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for pig heart, raw (cr_id F06090014). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this primary regional source. Saturated fat, fatty acid fractions, and extended micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB No. 10073 (Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, heart, raw) for completeness.

gi_source.title: N/A — Pig heart, raw contains negligible carbohydrates (1.33g/100g, predominantly glycogen); glycemic index is not applicable for organ meats with negligible digestible carbohydrate
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pig heart, raw has only 1.33g carbohydrate per 100g, primarily as glycogen. The University of Sydney GI Database does not list organ meats as GI-testable foods. GI is not applicable for this food category; the numeric value 0 is used as a sentinel to satisfy schema numeric requirements, with gi_category explicitly set to 'not_applicable' to communicate the true clinical interpretation. Glycemic load per serving is effectively 0.0 given negligible carbohydrate content.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (56mg/100g) and high potassium (294mg/100g) providing a favourable Na:K ratio of approximately 1:5.25
hypertension.guidance: Pig heart, raw is suitable for hypertensive individuals in its unprocessed state due to very low sodium and high potassium; however, cooking methods using soy sauce, oyster sauce, or salt-based brines will substantially increase sodium content and should be avoided or minimised.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate (1.33g/100g), zero sugar, and high protein (17.27g/100g) supporting glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pig heart, raw has a minimal glycaemic impact due to near-zero carbohydrate and zero sugar content; its high protein density supports satiety and glycaemic control, but individuals with T2D should monitor total cholesterol intake (131mg/100g) given elevated cardiovascular risk in this population and avoid sugar-based marinades during preparation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol (131mg/100g) and moderate arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in pork organ meat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While pig heart is relatively low in saturated fat (1.49g/100g) and contains beneficial potassium (294mg/100g) and B12 (7.9mcg/100g), its cholesterol content of 131mg/100g warrants moderation for individuals with established cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia; consumption should be limited to occasional portions and prepared without high-fat or high-sodium sauces.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High arachidonic acid (omega-6) content in pork organ meat amplifies pro-inflammatory eicosanoid pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Pig heart is not recommended as a regular food for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high arachidonic acid content which promotes TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory signalling; high iron (4.68mg/100g) may also contribute to oxidative stress in inflamed joints, and no compensatory anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, polyphenols, antioxidants) are present.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pig heart, raw (cr_id F06090014) is the uncooked cardiac muscle organ from the domestic pig (Sus scrofa domesticus), classified under meat and alternatives — pork organ meats. It presents as a dense, firm, deep-red muscle tissue with a smooth outer surface and visible coronary fat deposits. Raw pig heart has a mild, iron-rich aroma characteristic of fresh organ meats. When cooked, it develops a firm, slightly chewy texture with a savoury, mildly gamey flavour that is less pronounced than liver or kidney. It is widely consumed across Southeast Asia (Singapore, Malaysia, Vietnam), East Asia (China, Taiwan), and parts of Europe (Portugal, Germany) in preparations including braised dishes (lu wei), soups, stir-fries, and grilled skewers. Nutritionally, pig heart is a high-protein (17.27g/100g), low-fat (4.36g/100g), low-calorie (118 kcal/100g) organ meat with exceptional micronutrient density: iron (4.68mg/100g — approximately 26% of adult daily requirement), vitamin B12 (7.9mcg/100g — over 300% of RDI), niacin (7.54mg/100g), thiamine (0.43mg/100g), and potassium (294mg/100g). Sodium is very low at 56mg/100g in the raw state. Dietary cholesterol is moderate-to-high at 131mg/100g. Carbohydrate content is negligible at 1.33g/100g (primarily glycogen), making it glycaemic-index non-applicable. Clinically, pig heart is suitable for hypertensive and diabetic individuals in its raw/minimally processed form but should be consumed in moderation by those with cardiovascular disease or dyslipidaemia due to cholesterol content. It is not recommended for frequent consumption by individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid profile. Pig heart is gluten-free and dairy-free but is not halal (pork) or kosher.
common_user_queries: Is pork heart good for diabetics?
Is pig heart high in protein?
Is pig heart suitable for hypertension?
meal_context: Braised pig heart (lu wei / 卤味)
Stir-fried pig heart with ginger and spring onion

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F06090014, confidence 0.95 in source record). Saturated fat, fatty acid profile, and selected micronutrients cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central NDB 10073 (Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, heart, raw) which is an exact match. Both sources are on the approved source list. No estimation or analogy required for core macros.
notes: Sugar_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo source; pig heart contains no free sugars. Dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — naturally zero as this is an animal organ meat containing no plant-derived fibre. Trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/absent in fresh pork heart per USDA FoodData Central NDB 10073. Vitamin_a_mcg_rae, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: these values are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo entry for this food item; USDA FoodData Central NDB 10073 reports some of these values but the HPB source does not confirm them for the Singapore-specific record, so -1 is used to avoid unsupported imputation. Saturated fat (1.49g), monounsaturated fat (1.02g), polyunsaturated fat (0.96g), and selected B-vitamins and minerals cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central NDB 10073 (Pork, fresh, variety meats and by-products, heart, raw) as the HPB source does not report fatty acid fractions or full micronutrient panel. Glycemic index is set to numeric 0 as a schema-compliant sentinel value; gi_category is explicitly 'not_applicable' because carbohydrate content is negligible (1.33g/100g, primarily glycogen) and the University of Sydney GI Database does not test organ meats. Glycemic load per serving is 0.0. Pig heart is pork-derived and therefore not halal or kosher. Dietary tag 'keto' from input is retained as appropriate given negligible carbohydrate content. FoodEx2 code A0FKP and FoodOn URI confirmed against published ontology references; SNOMED CT has no applicable food-item concept for raw pig heart.
